questions.question: Why read a scaling policy closely if compliance with it isn't legally binding?
options: It is enforceable in EU courts
The framing shapes how a lab conceives of catastrophic risk and communicates trade-offs publicly — scaling policies are the primary public signal labs emit about catastrophic-risk posture
Regulators wrote it
It determines API pricing
questions.explanation: Nothing forces a lab to follow its own RSP, but reading it with specificity and skepticism is a practical skill for anyone whose work depends on frontier capabilities. The v3.0-vs-v2.0 diff is the useful unit.

questions.question: What is the AI R&D-4 threshold in RSP v3.0?
options: A model with 4 trillion parameters
A capability level where a model could automate a substantial fraction of AI research at competitive cost — crossing it requires Anthropic to publish an affirmative case before continued scaling
The fourth version of the safety team
A benchmark score of 4.0
questions.explanation: Claude Opus 4.6 does not cross it, but the announcement concedes "confidently ruling this out is becoming difficult." Lessons 6 (AAR) and 7 (RSI) feed directly into this threshold — automated alignment researchers clearing quality bars is evidence it's approaching.

questions.question: What does the two-tier mitigation structure (new in v3.0) require a reader to check?
options: Whether the model is open-source
Which column a commitment lives in — Anthropic-unilateral actions are promises; industry-wide recommendations (including RAND SL-4) are policy advocacy, not Anthropic commitments
The model's release date
Whether the policy is in PDF or HTML
questions.explanation: A security measure in the "industry-wide recommendation" column is Anthropic's hope, not its promise. The two-tier split didn't exist in v2, so the same-looking measure can mean very different things depending on its column.

questions.question: What was the sharpest regression from RSP v2 to v3.0?
options: Removing the safety team
Dropping the explicit 2023 pause commitment (training pauses until mitigations are ready) in favor of a softer "publish an affirmative case and proceed if adequate"
Increasing the price of Claude
Removing public access to the document
questions.explanation: SaferAI and other analysts flagged this directly. The pro-change argument: 2023's quantitative thresholds became unreachable as benchmarks re-scaled. The counter: a pause clause is a commitment device, and removing it removes the policy's credibility.

questions.question: What did SaferAI's rating change signify?
options: Anthropic's RSP improved from 1.9 to 2.2
A downgrade from 2.2 (v2) to 1.9 (v3.0), moving Anthropic from "moderate" to "weak" alongside OpenAI and DeepMind — a policy got less rigorous while looking more polished
A perfect 4.0 score
That the RSP became legally binding
questions.explanation: Downgrade factors: qualitative thresholds replacing quantitative ones, the removed pause commitment, "affirmative case" instead of specific R&D-4 measures, and review depending on Anthropic's own Safety Advisory Group with limited independent oversight.
